questions.question: A straight-beam procedure was qualified with a 1 in., 2.25 MHz transducer. Swapping in a 0.5 in., 5 MHz unit without authorization is prohibited primarily because the substitution changes:
options: the near field, beam spread, and sensitivity the procedure was qualified with
the color of the displayed waveform
questions.explanation: Element size and frequency are essential variables: they set near-field length, beam spread, and response to small reflectors. The examination is only valid with the parameters the procedure was qualified with, or a documented Level III-approved change.

questions.question: Using N = D²f/4v, the near field of a 0.5 in. (12.7 mm), 5 MHz probe in steel (5,920 m/s) is approximately:
questions.explanation: N = (0.0127 m)² × 5×10⁶ ÷ (4 × 5,920) ≈ 0.034 m — about 34 mm (1.3 in.). Reflectors inside that zone sit in the oscillating near field where amplitude evaluation is unreliable.

questions.question: A procedure directs you to verify sensitivity on a set of blocks with identical flat-bottom holes at increasing metal-travel distances. This set exists to:
options: relate echo amplitude to reflector size at one depth
map how a constant-size reflector's amplitude falls with depth, supporting DAC construction
questions.explanation: Constant hole size with varying travel is a distance-amplitude set: it characterizes amplitude versus depth for an equal reflector, which is exactly the information a DAC or TCG captures. Varying hole size at constant travel would be an area-amplitude set.

questions.question: During a lamination scan, the back-wall echo set at 80% FSH on sound material reads 36% FSH over one region after coupling is confirmed. Against a record-at-50%-loss criterion, the region:
options: is recordable — 36% is 45% of reference, a 55% loss
is not recordable — 36% is more than 20% FSH
is recordable only if a flaw echo also appears
requires no action because the back wall is still visible
questions.explanation: Remaining back reflection = 36 ÷ 80 = 45%, so the loss is 55% — past the 50% threshold. Back-reflection-loss criteria exist precisely because tilted or scattering laminations may produce no direct echo.

questions.question: An indication peaks at 64% FSH. Applying the 6 dB drop to trace its boundary, you mark the probe centerline where the echo falls to:
questions.explanation: Six decibels is half amplitude: 64% ÷ 2 = 32% FSH. At that point the beam centerline sits approximately over the reflector's edge, and working around the indication traces its extent.

questions.question: A code inspector asks how your straight-beam report supports repeatability. The strongest answer is that it records:
options: the examiner's overall impression of weld quality
instrument, transducer, and calibration-block identification, reference gain, procedure revision, coverage, and datum-referenced results
only indications that exceeded the acceptance criteria
the time the shift started and ended
questions.explanation: A report must let another qualified technician duplicate the examination: full system identification, settings, governing documents, coverage achieved, and locations measured from a permanent datum. Findings alone reproduce nothing.

questions.question: For a 70° shear wave (tan 70° = 2.747) examining 0.625 in. plate, the full skip distance is approximately:
questions.explanation: Skip = 2T·tan θ = 2 × 0.625 × 2.747 ≈ 3.43 in. The scan band must extend roughly this far from the weld for full second-leg coverage.

questions.question: On the IIW block, maximizing the echo from the 100 mm radius and reading the wedge against the engraved reference mark accomplishes what?
options: Locates the true beam index (exit) point, the origin for all skip and surface-distance measurements
Verifies the shear-wave velocity of the part
questions.explanation: The radius returns a maximum when the beam exits directly at the block's focal mark, exposing the wedge's actual index point. Wedge wear moves that point, and every location measurement is referenced to it.

questions.question: With a 60° wedge on 1.0 in. plate, an echo appears at 2.60 in. sound path. One leg is 2.00 in., so the reflector lies at a depth of:
questions.explanation: 2.60 in. exceeds one leg (1.0 ÷ cos 60° = 2.00 in.), so the beam is in its second leg: depth = 2T − s·cos θ = 2.00 − 2.60 × 0.5 = 0.70 in. below the scanning surface.

questions.question: A repeatable signal runs the full length of a single-V pipe weld, plots at the far surface slightly offset from the weld centerline, and weakens markedly when scanned from the opposite side. The most likely source is:
options: a continuous centerline crack in the root
counterbore or root geometry — confirmed by plotting against the joint detail before any disposition
porosity scattered through the weld volume
a lamination in the base material at mid-wall
questions.explanation: Far-surface position, constant offset, full-length consistency, and side-to-side asymmetry are the signature of machined counterbore or root geometry. It is proven nonrelevant by plotting against the drawing — and documented, not ignored.
